title: Nonhomogeneous Equations and Forced Response
subtitle: Finding particular solutions by every major technique, and understanding how systems respond to inputs.
description: Finding particular solutions by every major technique, and understanding how systems respond to inputs.

atAGlance.title: Unit 10 at a Glance: Core Sub-Topics
atAGlance.summary: A nonhomogeneous linear equation reads $L[y] = g(x)$, and its general solution always splits as $y = y_h + y_p$ --- the complementary solution of $L[y]=0$ plus \emph{any} particular solution of the forcing. This unit is the toolkit for building $y_p$ and for reading how a system answers an input.

bullets.body: guess a trial of the same shape as the forcing, then match coefficients.
promote the constants $c_1,c_2$ to functions for \emph{any} continuous forcing.
an operator that kills $g$ derives the trial form instead of memorizing it.
the homogeneous part decays, the particular part persists.
$u(t)$, $\delta(t)$, and the system's answer to a unit kick from rest.
convolve the impulse response with the input to answer \emph{every} forcing at once.

modules.title: The Method of Undetermined Coefficients
modules.topics: For a constant-coefficient $L[y] = g(x)$, the general solution is $y = y_h + y_p$: the complementary solution $y_h$ solves $L[y]=0$ and carries the arbitrary constants, while $y_p$ matches the forcing. When $g$ is an exponential, polynomial, sine/cosine, or a product of these, guess a trial of the \emph{same shape} and solve for its coefficients.

formulas.title: Complementary Plus Particular
formulas.body:
\[ y = y_h + y_p, \qquad L[y_h] = 0, \qquad L[y_p] = g(x). \]
Only $y_h$ holds the free constants, so it alone can absorb the initial or
boundary conditions --- which is why $y_p$ is never the whole story.

formulas.title: Trial Solution Table (No Overlap)
formulas.body:
\[
\begin{array}{l|l}
\strong{Forcing } g(x) & \strong{Trial } y_p \\ \hline
e^{\alpha x} & A e^{\alpha x} \\
\text{polynomial of degree } n & A_n x^n + \cdots + A_1 x + A_0 \\
\cos\beta x \ \text{ or } \ \sin\beta x & A\cos\beta x + B\sin\beta x \\
e^{\alpha x}\cos\beta x & e^{\alpha x}(A\cos\beta x + B\sin\beta x) \\
\end{array}
\]
For a \emph{sum} of forcings, build a particular solution for each piece and add
them (superposition for the nonhomogeneous equation).

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Overlap with the Homogeneous Solution
warning.body:
If any term of the trial already solves $L[y]=0$, substitution collapses it to
zero and the method fails. \strong{Multiply the offending trial by $x$} (or by a
higher power $x^k$ for a root of multiplicity $k$) until no term overlaps $y_h$.
For $y''+y=\cos x$, the bare guess $A\cos x + B\sin x$ \emph{is} the homogeneous
solution; the corrected resonant trial is $x(A\cos x + B\sin x)$.

modules.title: Variation of Parameters
modules.topics: When the forcing is not one of the friendly shapes --- $\tan x$, $\sec x$, $e^x/x$ --- undetermined coefficients has nothing to guess. Variation of parameters promotes the constants $c_1,c_2$ of $y_h = c_1 y_1 + c_2 y_2$ to functions $u_1(x),u_2(x)$ and works for \emph{any} continuous $g$.

formulas.title: Variation of Parameters Formulas
formulas.body:
Write the equation in standard form $y'' + p y' + q y = g$ (leading coefficient
one), let $W = y_1 y_2' - y_2 y_1'$ be the Wronskian, and set $y_p = u_1 y_1 + u_2 y_2$ with
\[ u_1' = -\frac{y_2\, g}{W}, \qquad u_2' = \frac{y_1\, g}{W}, \]
\[ y_p = -\,y_1\!\int \frac{y_2\, g}{W}\,dx \;+\; y_2\!\int \frac{y_1\, g}{W}\,dx. \]

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Forgetting to Normalize
warning.body:
The $g$ in these formulas is the right-hand side \emph{after} dividing through so
the $y''$ coefficient is one. Reading $g$ off an unnormalized equation scales the
answer incorrectly. Always normalize first, then read $g$.

modules.title: Differential Operators and the Annihilator Method
modules.topics: Writing $D = \tfrac{d}{dx}$, a constant-coefficient operator factors like a polynomial. An \strong{annihilator} of $g$ is an operator $A(D)$ with $A(D)g = 0$. Applying it to both sides of $L[y]=g$ turns the problem into a larger \emph{homogeneous} equation whose extra solutions are exactly the trial form.

formulas.title: Common Annihilators
formulas.body:
\[
\begin{array}{l|l}
\strong{Function} & \strong{Annihilator} \\ \hline
e^{\alpha x} & (D-\alpha) \\
x^{k-1} & D^{k} \\
\cos\beta x,\ \sin\beta x & (D^2 + \beta^2) \\
e^{\alpha x}\cos\beta x,\ e^{\alpha x}\sin\beta x & (D-\alpha)^2 + \beta^2 \\
\end{array}
\]
A repeated factor $(D-\alpha)^k$ signals the terms
$e^{\alpha x}, x e^{\alpha x}, \ldots, x^{k-1}e^{\alpha x}$. Annihilate a sum by
\emph{multiplying} the individual annihilators.

methodology.title: Annihilator Workflow
steps: Factor $L(D)$ and write the complementary solution $y_h$.
Pick the annihilator $A(D)$ of the forcing $g$.
Drop the terms already in $y_h$; the survivors are the trial $y_p$.

modules.title: Transient and Steady-State Response
modules.topics: For a \emph{stable} forced system (characteristic roots with negative real parts), the complete solution $y = y_h + y_p$ separates by long-term behavior: the homogeneous part fades while the particular part endures.

formulas.title: Transient Versus Steady-State
formulas.body:
\[ y(t) = \underbrace{y_h(t)}_{\text{transient}\ \to\ 0} \;+\; \underbrace{y_p(t)}_{\text{steady-state}}. \]
The \strong{transient} carries the initial-condition dependence and decays because
its exponentials have negative real parts. The \strong{steady-state} is fixed by
the forcing alone, so long after the start the response is essentially independent
of the initial data. For sinusoidal forcing the steady state is a sinusoid at the
\emph{drive} frequency, rescaled and phase-shifted, often read off the complex
gain $1/p(i\omega)$.

modules.title: Unit Step and Impulse Response
modules.topics: Two idealized inputs model switching and striking. The unit step $u(t)$ switches a forcing on; the Dirac delta $\delta(t)$ is an instantaneous unit kick. They are linked by calculus, and so are the responses they produce.

formulas.title: Step, Delta, and Their Responses
formulas.body:
\[ u(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t<0 \\ 1, & t>0 \end{cases}, \qquad
   \delta(t) = u'(t), \qquad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\delta(t)\,dt = 1. \]
\strong{Sifting property:} $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\,\delta(t-a)\,dt = f(a)$.
The \strong{impulse response} $w(t)$ is the output of $L[y]=\delta(t)$ from rest;
the \strong{step response} is its integral. Knowing $w$ unlocks every input by
convolution.

warning.title: Common Pitfall: Forgetting the Rest State
warning.body:
The impulse response is defined from \strong{rest} --- zero state just before the
kick. Starting with leftover energy mixes in a transient that is unrelated to the
impulse, so the clean $w(t)$ (here $w(0)=0$, $w'(0)=1$ for a second-order system)
is lost. Isolate the impulse by zeroing the initial conditions first.

modules.title: Duhamel's Principle
modules.topics: Duhamel's principle views a general input as a continuous succession of small impulses. Each kick at time $\tau$ produces a scaled, time-shifted copy of the impulse response, and superposition sums them into a convolution.

methodology.title: Duhamel's Convolution
steps: For a linear, time-invariant system starting from rest, the forced response to an input $f(t)$ is \[ y_p(t) = \int_0^t w(t-\tau)\,f(\tau)\,d\tau, \] where $w$ is the impulse response (the Green's function of the initial-value problem). The limits $0$ to $t$ encode \strong{causality} --- only inputs already applied can affect the present --- and the argument $t-\tau$ is the time elapsed since each kick. \strong{Linearity} and \strong{time invariance} are exactly what make the convolution valid.

concept.title: Unit 10 Key Takeaway
concept.body:
Every forced response is \highlight{$y = y_h + y_p$}. Build $y_p$ by
\highlight{undetermined coefficients} when the forcing is friendly
(remember to \highlight{multiply by $x$} on overlap), by
\highlight{variation of parameters} for anything continuous, or read
its form from an \highlight{annihilator}. In a stable system the
\highlight{transient} $y_h$ decays and the
\highlight{steady-state} $y_p$ remains, and once the
\highlight{impulse response $w$} is known,
\highlight{Duhamel's convolution} answers every input at once.

cheat.subtitle: Condensed Cheat Sheet: Nonhomogeneous Equations and Forced Response

takeaway.title: Unit 10 Key Takeaway
takeaway.body:
The shape of the forcing chooses the tool:
\highlight{undetermined coefficients} for exponentials,
polynomials, and sinusoids --- with the \highlight{$\times x$
resonance fix} whenever a trial term solves $L[y]=0$ ---
\highlight{variation of parameters} for $\sec x$, $e^x/x$, and other
unfriendly inputs, and the \highlight{annihilator} to derive the
trial form. In a stable system the \highlight{transient} decays to
the \highlight{steady-state}, and the
\highlight{impulse response $w(t)=e^{-t}-e^{-2t}$} feeds
\highlight{Duhamel's convolution} to resolve every input at once.
